rescuing.definition: Saving someone from a difficult situation by doing everything for them.
rescuing.example: Rescuing your child too much can stop them from learning.

insecure.definition: Not feeling confident or safe; worried about being rejected.
insecure.example: Children who are pushed too fast may become insecure.

clingy.definition: Wanting to stay very close and not let go.
clingy.example: A clingy child may just need more time to feel safe.

nursery.definition: A place where young children are cared for during the day.
nursery.example: She dropped her son off at nursery this morning.

nudge.definition: To gently push or encourage someone to do something.
nudge.example: You can nudge a child to try new things.

imposing.definition: Forcing something on someone without asking.
imposing.example: Imposing your pace on a child can cause problems.

trust.definition: To believe that someone is reliable and good.
trust.example: Trust your child to separate when they are ready.

crawl.definition: To move on hands and knees close to the ground.
crawl.example: The baby learned to crawl at eight months.

solids.definition: Foods that are not liquid, given to babies when they start eating.
solids.example: Most babies start eating solids around six months.

rent.definition: Money you pay to use a house or apartment.
rent.example: One day your child will grow up and pay their own rent.

frustrate.definition: To make someone feel annoyed because they cannot do something.
frustrate.example: Pushing a child too fast will frustrate them.

painstakingly.definition: Done with great care and effort over a long time.
painstakingly.example: She painstakingly taught her child to tie his shoes.

delay.definition: To make something happen later than planned.
delay.example: Rushing a child's development may actually delay it.

prop.definition: To hold something up or support it in a position.
prop.example: Don't prop the baby into a sitting position too early.

restrict.example: Props that restrict movement can slow down learning.

wriggle.definition: To twist and turn the body with small quick movements.
wriggle.example: Babies wriggle on the floor as they learn to move.

devices.definition: In 'left to their own devices': allowed to do things by themselves.
devices.example: Left to their own devices, children learn to solve problems.

lopsided.definition: Not even or balanced; heavier or bigger on one side.
lopsided.example: The baby learned a lopsided shuffle instead of crawling.

shuffle.definition: To move along by dragging your body or feet without lifting them properly.
shuffle.example: The baby did a sitting shuffle across the floor.

posture.definition: The way you hold your body when sitting or standing.
posture.example: Good posture helps prevent back pain.

fate.definition: What happens to someone, especially something they cannot control.
fate.example: This was the fate of my own daughter.

conscious.definition: Aware of something; knowing about it.
conscious.example: I am conscious that you might feel bad reading this.

Pilates.definition: A type of exercise that focuses on strength, flexibility, and posture.
Pilates.example: She does Pilates to improve her posture.

amends.definition: Something done to fix a mistake or make up for something wrong.
amends.example: Making amends for mistakes is more important than being perfect.

defensive.definition: Protecting yourself because you feel criticized or attacked.
defensive.example: Being defensive about mistakes makes them worse.

disempower.definition: To take away someone's power or ability to do things for themselves.
disempower.example: Don't disempower your child by doing everything for them.

specific.definition: Exact and clearly defined; about one particular thing.
specific.example: The sitting example is specific but makes a general point.

squawk.definition: A short, loud, rough sound like a bird makes.
squawk.example: The baby made a squawk when she couldn't reach the toy.

ping-pong.definition: A small, light ball used in table tennis.
ping-pong.example: The baby was trying to grab a ping-pong ball.

encouragingly.definition: In a way that gives someone hope and makes them want to try.

squeals.example: The baby squeals with delight when she gets the toy.

delight.definition: A feeling of great happiness and pleasure.

scenario.definition: A situation that might happen; a possible event.
scenario.example: In this scenario, the father helped just enough.

rescue.definition: To save someone who is in trouble or danger.
rescue.example: If you rescue them too often, they won't learn.

cues.definition: Signs or signals that tell you something.
cues.example: Watch your baby for cues about what they need.

robbing.example: Doing too much for children is robbing them of agency.

agency.definition: The power to make your own choices and act on them.
agency.example: Children need to develop their own sense of agency.

sensitive.definition: Aware of other people's feelings and needs.
sensitive.example: Be sensitive towards your child when they are struggling.

adopt.definition: To start using a new way of doing things.
adopt.example: It's a good idea to adopt this parenting style.

exercise.definition: An activity done for practice or to improve a skill.
exercise.example: Try this exercise to build a better relationship with your child.

problem-solve.definition: To find answers to difficult questions or situations.
problem-solve.example: Help your child problem-solve instead of fixing it for them.
